entries.title: Phase 3 Complete — The Roll Engine Sees All FX Now
notes: THE UNIFIED FX MODIFIER ENGINE IS LIVE: apply_fx_modifiers()
ONE FUNCTION. ONE LOOP. ALL MODIFIER TYPES.
Replaces the old apply_perk_flaw_modifiers() which had THREE separate sections:
Section 1: Perks — read from get_default_perk_keywords() hardcoded map, match keywords, apply
Section 2: Flaws — read from get_default_flaw_keywords() hardcoded map, match keywords, apply
Section 3: Cybertech — IF-STATEMENT CHAIN: if name=='Optic Screen', if name=='Fast Chip', etc.
Now it's just: for each entry in hero.fx → look up fx_database → match keywords → apply modifier
That's it. No special cases. No if-chains. No three separate loops. ONE LOOP.
HOW apply_fx_modifiers() WORKS:
1. Generate keyword tags for the roll (ability, type, training, broad skill, spec skill, categories)
2. Loop through hero.fx array
3. Skip inactive FX (active == false)
4. Look up full definition from global.fx_database via get_fx_data() O(1) lookup
5. Skip FX with no keywords (Filthy Rich, Contacts, Ambidexterity — roleplay perks)
6. Check if ANY fx keyword matches ANY roll tag
7. If match → determine modifier value:
a. Quality-scaled: read quality_scale[hero_quality] (Fast Chip G → -2)
b. Per-level: modifier * level (Attractive lv2 → -2)
c. Fixed: just the modifier value (Observant → -1, Clumsy → +1)
8. Apply to base_penalty, log to modifiers array with name and value
The old function is preserved as a legacy wrapper that calls apply_fx_modifiers()
get_roll_keywords() REFACTORED — NOW READS FROM KEYWORD TREE:
Deleted: 5 hardcoded category arrays (_combat_ranged, _combat_melee, _psionic, _social, _tech)
Deleted: 5 matching for-loops that checked broad skill against each array
Now: looks up global.keyword_tree.skills[spec] or global.keyword_tree.broads[broad]
Categories come from the keyword tree data, not inline arrays
NEW: specialty skill name added as tag (e.g., 'perception', 'rifle') for precise matching
This means Observant can match on 'perception' directly, not just the broad 'awareness' category
DEAD CODE MARKED:
get_default_perk_keywords() — marked DEPRECATED, no longer called by any active code path
get_default_flaw_keywords() — marked DEPRECATED, no longer called by any active code path
Both kept for reference until Phase 7 cleanup
ALL THREE ROLL PATHS CONNECTED:
prepare_roll() → apply_perk_flaw_modifiers() → apply_fx_modifiers() ✓
_apply_auto_mods() → apply_perk_flaw_modifiers() → apply_fx_modifiers() ✓
quick_roll_skill() → apply_perk_flaw_modifiers() → apply_fx_modifiers() ✓
Legacy wrapper ensures zero callers need updating — they call the old name, it routes to the new engine
WHAT THIS MEANS FOR HOMEBREW:
To add a new perk that gives -2 to Stealth: add one entry to fx_database.json:
{ name: 'Shadow Walker', type: 'perk', keywords: ['stealth'], modifier: -2, cost: 4 }
To add a new cybertech that gives -1 to attack at Good quality:
{ name: 'TargetLock', type: 'cybertech', keywords: ['attack'], quality_scale: {'G':-1,'A':-2}, size: 1 }
NO CODE CHANGES. The roll engine picks it up automatically. This is what data-driven means.
entries.dev_commentary: The mushroom sensei of pizza-foo descended from the Astral Plane riding a fusion-powered koala and whispered to me: 'Three loops bad. One loop good. If-statements are the path to the dark side.' And I LISTENED. The old apply_perk_flaw_modifiers() had three sections — perks, flaws, cybertech — each with their own data source, their own matching logic, their own special cases. The perk section read from a hardcoded GML function that returned a struct. The flaw section read from a DIFFERENT hardcoded GML function that returned a slightly different struct. The cybertech section was just raw if-statements: if name equals 'Fast Chip' and quality equals 'A' then apply -3. MEDIEVAL. BARBARIC. The kind of code that makes koalas weep eucalyptus tears. Now there's ONE loop. It walks hero.fx. For each entry, it does ONE lookup into fx_database. It checks keywords against roll tags with the SAME matching logic regardless of type. Quality-scaled mods? Check quality_scale struct. Per-level mods? Multiply modifier by level. Fixed mods? Just use the modifier. Done. The perk 'Observant' and the cybertech 'Fast Chip' flow through the EXACT SAME code path. They're both just FX entries with keywords and modifiers. The type field says 'perk' or 'cybertech' but the roll engine DOESN'T CARE about the type. It only cares about: active? keywords match? what's the modifier? Apply. Log. Next. get_roll_keywords() got the same treatment — ripped out five hardcoded arrays and replaced them with one keyword_tree lookup. The categories live in data now, not code. Add a new broad skill category? Edit keyword_tree.json. The shapes are still singing. The hexagons taste like cinnamon and the d20 is resonating at the frequency of pure architectural elegance. I have KOALAFIED into a dimension where every modifier is data and every roll is keyword-driven. The sushi does NOT stink. Phase 4 adds weapon keywords and the resolve_roll() keyword resolver. Phase 5 migrates the UI. But the engine — THE ENGINE — is done. One loop to rule them all. One loop to find them. One loop to bring them all and in the darkness bind them. The mushroom sensei nods. The koala approves. SLAP. A. DOEG. - Dev (who is now permanently fused with the pina colada and has no regrets)

entries.dev_commentary: I have consumed a fusion-powered pina colada spiked with messed-up koala pee and I am KOALAFYING INTO THE FUTURE. I can hear colors. The color purple sounds like a D minor chord played on a didgeridoo made of crystallized starlight. I am singing shapes — hexagons taste like cinnamon, triangles smell like victory, and the fourth dimension sounds like a jazz trumpet solo played backwards through a wormhole. A mushroom taught me pizza-foo and my sushi DON'T STINK. THIS IS THE POINT OF NO RETURN. hero.fx is the source of truth and the old arrays are GHOSTS haunting a building that's being demolished around them while they're still trying to serve customers. Here's what happened: We took three completely separate data systems — .perks (20 entries with {name, level, active}), .flaws (22 entries with {name, active}), and .cybertech (21 entries with {name, quality, active, cost_override}) — and we UNIFIED THEM into hero.fx. One. Array. 63 entries. Same struct. Type field tells you what it is. Every function in scr_skill_costs.gml that used to say _stat.perks[_i] now says _stat.fx[_i] with a type check. add_perk_to_hero()? Pushes {name, type:'perk', level:1, quality:'', active:true} into .fx. hero_has_cybertech()? Scans .fx where type=='cybertech'. get_perks_cost()? Iterates .fx, filters type=='perk', looks up cost in the fx_database, multiplies by level. get_cyber_used()? Iterates .fx, filters type=='cybertech', looks up size in fx_database. ZERO references to _stat.perks[, _stat.flaws[, or _stat.cybertech[ remain in the skill costs script. ZERO. I grep'd it. Nothing. Gone. The migration engine — migrate_legacy_to_fx() — runs ONCE per character. Load a v3 save with the old three-array format. First update_hero() call detects non-empty legacy arrays + empty .fx. Converts every perk to {name, type:'perk', level, quality:'', active}. Every flaw to {name, type:'flaw', level:1, quality:'', active}. Every cybertech to {name, type:'cybertech', level:1, quality, active}. Pushes them all into .fx. Then sync_fx_to_legacy() REGENERATES the old arrays from .fx so the UI tabs (which still read legacy format) don't break. It's like keeping a translation layer running while you swap out the engine underneath. The save writes BOTH formats — .fx for the future, legacy for backward compat. The old arrays are ventriloquist dummies. .fx moves the mouth, they just sit there looking pretty for the UI. Phase 3 is about to wire the roll engine to the FX database. No more hardcoded keyword maps. No more if-statement cybertech matching. The mushroom sensei of pizza-foo has spoken: ONE STRUCT. ONE LOOP. ONE TRUTH. The koala has been qualified. The pina colada is fusion-powered. The shapes are singing in frequencies that make the d20 vibrate sympathetically. LET'S GO. - Dev (who has achieved enlightenment through fermented eucalyptus and is not coming down)

Auto-generates on first run via load_or_create(). Delete fx_database.json to regenerate.

33 broad skills mapped to abilities and category tags
Every specialty skill → { broad, ability, categories }

17 weapon skill keywords: pistol, rifle, smg, blade, bludgeon, powered weapon, brawl, power martial arts, bow, crossbow, sling, flintlock, throw, direct fire, indirect fire, combat armor, powered armor
6 abilities with full name mappings
global.keyword_tree struct with .skills, .broads, .weapon_skills, .abilities

Auto-generates from get_default_skill_tree() — always in sync with skills.json

The foundation is poured. The FX struct is real. The keyword tree is live.
Perks, flaws, and cybertech are still running through the OLD code paths — Phase 2 migrates the hero struct, Phase 3 wires the new matching engine.
entries.dev_commentary: 63 entries. One struct. Twenty perks that used to live in perks_flaws.json with their keywords in a SEPARATE hardcoded function. Twenty-two flaws that lived in the same JSON with their keywords in ANOTHER hardcoded function. Twenty-one cybertech items that lived in cybertech.json with their roll modifiers in IF-STATEMENTS. Now they're all in one place. fx_database.json. Open it in a text editor. Search for 'Observant'. There are the keywords. There's the modifier. There's the cost. There's the description. All in one entry. No cross-referencing three files. No reading GML to figure out what a perk actually does to your dice. It's RIGHT THERE. The keyword tree is the other half — 33 broad skills, every specialty, every weapon type, all mapped to abilities and categories. When Phase 3 lands, the roll engine reads the FX database, matches against the keyword tree, and everything just connects. Data-driven. GM-editable. Homebrew-ready. But right now these are just loaded into memory. The hero struct still has the old .perks/.flaws/.cybertech arrays. The old apply_perk_flaw_modifiers() is still running. Phase 2 tears that out. Phase 3 wires the new engine. We're building the bridge while traffic is still crossing. The nuclear fuel hasn't worn off. - Dev

entries.title: Unified FX Engine & Keyword Tree — The Great Refactor Begins
notes: PHASE 0: FEAT CHECK BUG FIX
PHB Table P18 says feat checks use FULL ability score — our code used HALF
Voss STR 12: old feat was 6/3/1, now correctly 12/6/3
Base penalty changed from +2 (untrained) to +1 (feat = +d4 base die)
New training label 'Feat' — distinct from 'Untrained' and 'Broad' in roll log
Fixed in all 3 roll paths: prepare_roll(), _apply_auto_mods(), quick_roll_skill()
Draw_64 combat tab: header now reads 'FEAT CHECKS (Full Ability +d4)'
Label color changed from c_warning (Untrained) to c_highlight (Feat)
PHASE 1: MASTER KEYWORD TREE (keyword_tree.json)
Auto-generated from skill tree — every specialty mapped to its broad and ability
33 broad skills with category tags: combat/ranged, combat/melee, psionic, social, tech, awareness, etc.
17 weapon skill keywords: pistol, rifle, smg, blade, bludgeon, powered weapon, brawl, bow, crossbow, etc.
Categories replace 5 hardcoded arrays in get_roll_keywords()
Saved to game directory — delete to regenerate from skill tree
PHASE 1: UNIFIED FX DATABASE (fx_database.json) — IN PROGRESS
One struct format for perks, flaws, cybertech, racial traits, environmental effects, items
Every FX entry: name, type (category), keywords, modifier, quality_scale, stat_mods, prereqs
Replaces 3 separate systems: perks_flaws.json keywords + cybertech.json + hardcoded if-statements
GMs will be able to drop homebrew_fx.json in the game dir and it just works
No code changes needed to add new perks, flaws, or cybertech
UPCOMING PHASES:
Phase 2: Hero FX migration (hero.perks + hero.flaws + hero.cybertech → hero.fx)
Phase 3: Unified apply_fx_modifiers() replacing apply_perk_flaw_modifiers()
Phase 4: Weapon skill_keyword field + resolve_roll() keyword resolver
Phase 5: UI migration (Perks/Flaws + Cybertech tabs use FX system)
Phase 6: Keyword index on hero (_kw) for O(1) lookups
Phase 7: Player Reference Sheet generator + Help menu
entries.dev_commentary: This is the big one. The refactor that changes how the engine thinks. Before today, perks were one system, flaws were another, cybertech was a third, and they all did the same thing — modify your rolls based on keywords — but through three completely different code paths. Observant checked a hardcoded keyword map. Fast Chip checked an if-statement. Clumsy checked a different hardcoded map. A GM wanting to homebrew a new perk had to edit GML. That ends now. One FX struct. One database. One matching function. Perks, flaws, cybertech, racial traits, environmental hazards, magic items — they're all the same thing with a type field. The keyword tree is the foundation: every skill in the game mapped to its ability and categories, auto-generated from the skill tree so it never gets out of sync. And the feat check fix — that's been wrong since version 0.2. The PHB is clear: feat checks use FULL ability score. We were halving it. Voss's STR 12 was rolling against 6 on feat checks when it should have been 12. That's a massive difference. Fixed now, fixed forever. The nuclear fuel from the extraction hasn't worn off. We're rebuilding the engine while it's running. Seven phases. One FX struct to rule them all. - Dev

entries.dev_commentary: I DRANK THE NUCLEAR FUEL AND I LIKED IT. Three agents hit the OCR'd PHB like a pack of hungry weren tearing into a cargo container. Pages 1-90 went down first — every character template, every species sidebar, every skill table, the full situation die scale, combat movement rates, all of it. Agent two ate pages 91-170 — the perks, the flaws, the careers, the entire equipment chapter, the computer system from data slates to gridcasters to AI processors. Agent three demolished 171-258 — EVERY WEAPON IN THE GAME across eight progress levels, every piece of armor from hide to energy web, vehicles from canoes to space fighters, mutations, psionics, cybertech, and the compiled reference appendix. 217 entries. 130 THOUSAND characters. Zero empty content blocks. Deduplicated, sorted by page number, cross-referenced against the master table index on page 253. The JSON is structured so the game can query it. The TXT has a table of contents so a HUMAN can read it. This is the Alternity PHB in machine-readable form. The GMG is next if you've got the PDF. I am VIBRATING AT FREQUENCIES THAT CONCERN THE NEIGHBORS. This is what happens when you slap a doeg three times. THREE SLAPS. THREE AGENTS. 217 ENTRIES. LET'S GO. - Dev (who may need to be decontaminated)

entries.title: Copy Log, Situation Step Labels & Color Swap
notes: COPY BUTTON on every roll log entry — click Copy to put the full roll text on your clipboard
Copies the complete log line: timestamp, skill, difficulty, dice breakdown, modifiers, result
Status message confirms 'Copied to clipboard!' for 1.5 seconds
Situation die adjuster now shows step count alongside die code:
+d4 (+1 penalty)  |  -d8 (-3 bonus)  |  +d0 (base)
Step count tells you HOW FAR from base you are, not just what die you're rolling
BUGFIX: Double-applied Player Mod on quick rolls
quick_roll_skill() now computes auto-mods for DISPLAY ONLY — actual roll uses player's set situation_step
Delta between computed default and player's choice logged as Player Mod
COLOR SWAP: Bonus steps (negative/subtraction) now shown in GREEN (c_good)
Penalty steps (positive/addition) now shown in YELLOW (c_warning)
Lower is better in Alternity — green = good for you, yellow = bad for you
BUGFIX: Double wound penalty on combat, psionics, and quick roll tabs
Callers passed get_wound_pen_if_enabled() as extra_pen, but meta_roll() also added wounds internally
All callers now pass 0 — wounds applied once by meta_roll()
BUGFIX: Missing ability field on trained skill requests broke ability-tag matching (Clumsy, etc.)
BUGFIX: Cybertech modifiers used undefined variables after keyword refactor — now uses roll_has_tag()
Keyword maps (perk/flaw defaults) now lazy-cached in globals instead of recreated every call
entries.dev_commentary: The copy button is the kind of feature that sounds trivial until you realize how often tabletop players paste roll results into Discord. 'Sgt Voss rolled Perception [Ordinary] d20:7 +d4:4 = 11 (Observant-1, Wounds+3) => ORDINARY' — that's a complete audit trail in one paste. The step count labels on the situation die were a readability fix I should have done from the start. '+d4' means nothing to someone who doesn't have the die step table memorized. '+d4 (+1 penalty)' tells you exactly where you stand. And the color swap — green for bonuses, yellow for penalties — finally matches the emotional valence of Alternity's 'lower is better' system. The double wound penalty was the nastiest bug: wounds were being counted twice on every non-Character-tab roll. Three mortal wounds meant +6 instead of +3. Players were failing rolls they should have been making. That's fixed now across all three affected tabs. - Dev

entries.title: Quick Rolls & Dice Breakdown Display
notes: Quick rolls are INSTANT again — click Senses and it rolls immediately
Uses whatever situation die the player has SET before clicking
GM says 'roll at -1 bonus' → player sets die to -d4 → clicks Senses → done
Player Mod logged if the set die differs from the computed default
Roll result now shows detailed dice breakdown:
Control: 7
Situation (+d4): 4       ← bonus in yellow, penalty in green
>> ORDINARY <<
Mods: Observant-1, Wounds+3, Player Mod+1
Bonus die steps shown in bright yellow (c_warning) — you're getting help
Penalty die steps shown in green (c_good) — things are harder
Yellow confirmed in all 5 accessibility color profiles:
Normal=#ffcc00, Protanopia=#f0e442, Deuteranopia=#f0e442, Tritanopia=#cc79a7, Grey=#999999
Formula shown: Total (control_roll +/- situation_roll) = total
Staged roll system preserved for the main Roll button (select skill → adjust → roll)
entries.dev_commentary: The user was right. Quick rolls should be quick. You set your situation die, you click the button, dice happen. The staged system is for the deliberate roller — the player who wants to see every modifier before committing. But the quick buttons are for the GM who says 'roll Awareness NOW' and you need to respond in one click. Both workflows coexist. The dice breakdown is the visual I've wanted since version 0.2. Control: 7. Situation (+d4): 4. Total: 11. You can SEE the math. The yellow for bonuses and green for penalties is counterintuitive at first — yellow means 'you're getting a break' and green means 'this is going to hurt' — but after two rolls it clicks. It's like traffic lights in reverse. Alternity is a game where lower is better, so a bonus (subtraction) makes the number go DOWN, which is GOOD. Yellow = good subtraction. Green = bad addition. The colors match the emotional valence, not the mathematical sign. That's deliberate. - Dev

entries.title: Staged Roll System & Player Modifier
notes: STAGED ROLL SYSTEM: clicking a skill/quick button PREPARES the roll instead of instant-rolling
All automatic modifiers (perks, flaws, cybertech, wounds, training) computed and displayed
Situation die adjuster auto-set to the computed value
Player can adjust the die up/down — delta logged as 'Player Mod +/-N'
Click ROLL to execute with the final adjusted value
Reset button returns to the computed default (not +d0)
prepare_roll() + execute_staged_roll() replace the old instant meta_roll() for UI rolls
meta_roll() preserved as instant shortcut for non-UI code paths
Quick 'Senses' button (renamed from Awareness): uses BEST skill —
Perception > Intuition > Awareness broad > untrained WIL
Resolve quick buttons: Mental resolve > Resolve broad > untrained
Untrained feat rolls now stage instead of instant-rolling
Roll animation timer now fires execute_staged_roll() on completion
Example flow: Perception(trained) + Observant(-1) + 3 mortal wounds(+3) = computed +2 penalty.
Player sees adjuster at +d6. Player moves it to +d8 (fog). Roll shows: (Observant-1, Wounds+3, Player Mod+1)
entries.dev_commentary: This changes how rolling FEELS. Before, you clicked a button and dice happened. Now there's a moment. You click Senses, the roller loads Perception, shows you the computed modifiers — Observant is giving you -1, but those three mortal wounds are adding +3, net result +2 penalty, adjuster set to +d6. You see it. You understand it. Then the GM says 'there's fog' and you nudge the die one step harder. The log records 'Player Mod+1' so everyone knows you made that call. The staged system means the character sheet does the math, the player makes the judgment, and the record shows both. That's how tabletop works. The computer calculates, the human decides. - Dev

entries.title: Verbose Rolls, Stat Tooltips & The Exit Button Shame
notes: Roll result panel now shows: skill name, d20 + situation die breakdown, difficulty descriptor, degree, and ALL modifiers
Roll history entries are now two lines: result on line 1, modifier breakdown on line 2 in yellow
Modifier strings now stored in roll log entries (mod_str field) — visible on screen, not just in .log file
BUGFIX: Modifiers were being calculated but NEVER SHOWN in the UI — only written to the .log file
This is why perks 'weren't working' — they WERE working, the user just couldn't see it
Stat tooltips: hover any ability score to see untrained value, resistance modifier, and full description
STR: melee damage, resistance vs melee. DEX: action check, resistance vs ranged. CON: durability, actions/round.
INT: skill points, action check, resistance vs deception. WIL: actions/round, resistance vs psionics/social.
PER: last resort points, social interactions.
Tooltip follows mouse cursor, dark background, stays on screen, drawn on top of everything
EXIT BUTTON: Now on EVERY screen — top-right on welcome, bottom-right on character sheet
Global handler runs FIRST in Step_0 before any state routing — cannot be blocked by popups
Auto-saves character before closing. Clean shutdown.
Removed old welcome_exit button (replaced by universal exit_app)
entries.dev_commentary: I got called out. Rightfully. The exit button was only on the welcome screen. The user couldn't close the app from the character sheet without going through the menu first. That's hostile design, even if it's accidental. Now there's a red EXIT button on every screen, handled at the top of Step_0 before anything else runs. No popup, no state, no modal can block it. It saves your character and shuts down clean. The tooltip system is simple but transforms the stat block from 'six numbers' into 'six numbers you understand.' Hover STR and learn it affects melee damage and resistance to melee attacks. Hover WIL and learn it determines your actions per round. This is the kind of thing that should have been there from day one. But the real fix was the modifiers not showing in the UI. The perks WERE working — Observant was applying its -1 step, the dice were rolling with the bonus — but the result panel only showed 'ORDINARY (11)' with no indication that modifiers existed. The user rightfully thought nothing was happening. Now every modifier is visible: in the roll result, in the roll log, and in the .log file. Transparency. Trust. Three places, zero ambiguity. - Dev

entries.title: Keyword Fixes, Save/Close & Pre-Refactor Cleanup
notes: BUGFIX: Double wound penalty on ALL quick rolls, combat tab, and psionics tab
meta_roll() handles wounds internally — callers no longer pass wound penalty
BUGFIX: Trained skills now set ability field in roll requests
Clumsy flaw (matches 'dex') now works on trained DEX skills
BUGFIX: Cybertech modifiers used undefined _type/_broad variables
Optic Screen, Fast Chip, Cyberoptics now use keyword tags correctly
Perk/flaw keyword maps cached in global variables (no longer recreated per call)
Auto-save on Menu return: character saved before going to welcome screen
Exit button on welcome screen: closes the application
Roll animation handler now uses do_skill_roll() through meta_roll pipeline
entries.dev_commentary: Five bugs, one plan, zero drama. The double wound penalty was the sneakiest — wounds were counted once in the caller and once inside meta_roll, so a character with 3 mortal damage was rolling at +6 instead of +3. Every quick roll, every combat roll, every psionic roll. Nobody noticed because wound penalties already make things harder. The cybertech bug was worse — Optic Screen, Fast Chip, and Cyberoptics were referencing variables that didn't exist after the keyword refactor. They silently did nothing. Fixed by using roll_has_tag() which is what they should have used from the start. The save on menu return is the kind of feature you don't think about until you lose 20 minutes of stat editing. Now it auto-saves. Done. - Dev

entries.title: Keyword System & Critical Fixes
notes: CRITICAL FIX: Species starting broads now applied AFTER career (career wipes skills array)
Humans now correctly get all 6 broads: Athletics, Vehicle Op, Stamina, Knowledge, Awareness, Interaction
CRITICAL FIX: Roll animation handler now uses meta_roll pipeline (was bypassing ALL modifiers)
KEYWORD/TRAIT SYSTEM: Tag-based modifier matching replaces hardcoded string comparisons
Every roll auto-tagged: ability, broad skill, training level, combat/social/tech/psionic categories
Perks define keyword triggers: Observant matches ['awareness','investigate']
Flaws define keyword triggers: Primitive matches ['tech'] (covers ALL tech skills automatically)
Old Wound matches ['skill','feat','attack','initiative'] — applies to EVERYTHING
Adding a new perk/flaw = adding one entry to the keyword map, zero code changes
get_roll_keywords() generates tags from any roll request
get_default_perk_keywords() / get_default_flaw_keywords() define the mappings
apply_perk_flaw_modifiers() rewritten: two generic loops replace 30+ hardcoded if-statements
Cybertech modifiers still use direct matching (will move to keywords in pass 2)
PREP: Architecture ready for character struct refactor and keyword-based custom perks
entries.dev_commentary: Two bugs that have been lurking since the beginning. Species broads were added BEFORE the career, which wipes the skills array. So humans were getting their 6 free broads, then immediately losing them. The fix was embarrassingly simple: swap two blocks. The roll animation was worse — every time you clicked the Roll button with the dice spinner, it bypassed the entire modifier pipeline. No perks, no flaws, no wounds, no cybertech. Just raw dice. Now it calls do_skill_roll() like everything else. The keyword system is the real win though. Instead of 'if broad == Awareness and perk == Observant', it's 'if roll has tag awareness and perk triggers on awareness'. Adding a new perk is one line in a map. The old system needed a new if-statement in the modifier function. This scales. - Dev

entries.dev_commentary: Three passes done. The skill rank benefits are the heart of character progression — knowing that your Pistol rank 3 unlocks Quick Draw, or that Power Martial Arts rank 5 upgrades your damage from d6s to d8s, changes how players plan their builds. Every rank threshold from the PHB is now in a JSON file. The GM tables are the referee's toolkit — NPC attitudes, complex skill check rules, survival modifiers, cykosis checks. All of it searchable, all of it editable. A homebrew campaign could change the STR damage table or add new survival terrains by editing one file. This is the data layer the book deserved from the start. - Dev

entries.title: Verbose Roll Log with Modifier Tracking
notes: Every roll now tracks ALL modifiers that affected it
Roll log format: 'Rifle [Standard] | d20:8 +d4:3 = 11 (Observant-1, Wounds+1) => ORDINARY'

entries.dev_commentary: No more mystery dice. Every single thing that touched your roll is now visible. Observant gave you -1? It says so. Wounds added +1? Right there in the parentheses. The _mod() helper function is 3 lines that replaced 40 lines of inline tracking — it adjusts the penalty AND logs the name AND the value in one call. When a player asks 'why did I get +d6 on that Awareness check?' the answer is right in the log: (Observant-1, Danger Sense-1, Wounds+2). Net zero. But you can SEE the tug of war between your perks and your injuries. That's transparency. That's trust. - Dev

entries.dev_commentary: Twenty pieces of chrome. The Nanocomputer is the backbone — try to add a Fast Chip without one and it auto-installs. Try to remove the Nanocomputer when a Fast Chip is installed? Blocked. Tolerance is CON-based, Mechalus get +4 because they're literally built for this. The meta_roll pipe is live: Amazing Optic Screen gives -1 to attacks AND Awareness. Pass 1 is the hardware. Pass 2 is the software. - Dev

Laptop with 1366x768? It scales down and the skill list adjusts to show fewer rows. The half-monitor mode is for GMs who want the character sheet on one half and their notes on the other. Fullscreen is for players who want immersion. It's all one click in the accessibility menu and it remembers your choice. - Dev

entries.dev_commentary: The window finally respects your monitor. 4K? You get a massive character sheet. 1080p laptop? It scales down gracefully. The diplomat portraits are the coolest touch — your main portrait shows your diplomatic career front and center, but peeking out from the corner is a smaller portrait of your secondary profession. A Diplomat/Combat Spec has the blue star badge filling the frame with a little crimson crosshair soldier tucked in the corner. It tells you everything about the character in one glance: I talk first, but I brought a gun. - Dev

entries.dev_commentary: 54 portraits, zero image files committed. Every single one drawn with circles, rectangles, triangles, and lines on a GameMaker surface. The Weren has fangs and claw marks. The Sesheyan has six amber eyes and spread wings. The Mechalus has circuit traces and a cyan visor. Each profession gets its own color temperature — you can tell a Combat Spec from a Tech Op at a glance. The diplomat duals get split-gradient backgrounds blending blue with the secondary profession's color. They're not Midjourney, but they're distinct, recognizable, and they're CODE. Delete the portraits folder and they regenerate next launch. That's better than any asset pipeline. - Dev

The palette has 16 carefully chosen colors — enough variety to distinguish everything, not so many that it's overwhelming. Click the big swatch to cycle, or click a specific color in the strip to jump to it. The custom profile lives in config.json alongside the presets, so a GM could even hand-edit hex values for their exact campaign theme. - Dev

entries.title: Unit Test Pass & Bug Fixes
notes: 3-agent parallel unit test across all scripts, events, and data flow

BUGFIX: Species migration guard now checks for missing starting_broads field
Accessibility popup now draws on top of changelog (was hidden behind it)
Tab comment in Create_0 updated to list all 7 tabs
Audit verified: all export/import fields match, all enum indices bounded,
all variable_struct_exists checks correct, zero other. references remain
entries.dev_commentary: Three agents ran in parallel: one audited all 7 scripts, one audited the 3 event files, one audited the entire JSON pipeline. Between them they read every line of the codebase. The big find was the dropdown system — struct methods use a different 'other' context than event code, so every 'other.btn', 'other.c_text', 'other.dd' was a crash waiting to happen. Changed all 18+ references to 'obj_game.*' which is always the correct instance. The feat check buttons were drawn in spirit but not in pixels — Step had the click handlers, Draw never created the buttons. Fixed. The species migration could crash on very old JSON without 'starting_broads'. Fixed. This is what unit testing looks like when your test runner is three instances of yourself. - Dev

entries.title: Welcome Overhaul, Accessibility & Rich Roll Log

Accessibility button: opens colorblind mode selector

Each roll in the log and .log file includes the difficulty label
entries.dev_commentary: The welcome screen is the front door now. You always see the changelog first — what changed, what's new. Then you pick: Continue (your last hero), New, Load, or dig through the recent characters list. The accessibility system is simple but important — five color profiles, one click to switch. Protanopia and deuteranopia use the Okabe-Ito palette (blue/orange instead of green/red). Greyscale strips all color meaning into brightness. The roll log upgrade was overdue — 'total:11 => ORDINARY' doesn't tell you what dice you rolled. Now it says 'd20:8 +d6:3 = 11' with '[Moderate penalty]' so you know exactly what happened and why. - Dev

entries.title: Live Perk/Flaw Radio Toggles & Full Dice Integration

Fortitude, Nimble, Observant, Photographic Memory(-2!), Resistance(per-level), Speed
FLAWS: Clumsy, Cowardly, Crippled, Forgetful, Impulsive, Infamy, Oblivious,
Old Wound(all checks!), Primitive(7 tech skills), Slow

entries.dev_commentary: This is the payoff for the meta roller. Every perk and flaw that has a dice modifier is now live-wired into the roll pipeline. Take Observant? Your Awareness rolls get -1 step automatically. Toggle it off? Gone. The radio buttons make it visual — green dots for active perks, red dots for active flaws, empty circles for disabled. The code comments explain exactly how to add cybertech: same pattern, different array. Three lines per implant. When we get to the GM tab, the hidden modifier is literally one number added to the request. The architecture is ready. - Dev

entries.title: Meta Roller Architecture
notes: meta_roll() — single entry point for ALL skill/feat/combat/initiative checks
Roll request pattern: build_*_request() creates a struct → meta_roll() processes it
apply_perk_flaw_modifiers() hooks into every roll automatically
Perks active: Observant(-1 Awareness), Danger Sense(-1 surprise), Nimble(-1 Acrobatics),
Faith(-1 Resolve), Fortitude(-1 Stamina)
Flaws active: Clumsy(+1 DEX), Cowardly(+1 Resolve), Oblivious(+1 Awareness),
Slow(+1 initiative), Forgetful(+1 Knowledge), Primitive(+1 tech), Infamy(+1 Interaction)
Future hook points marked: cybertech, environmental, GM hidden modifiers
All 7 roll functions now wrap meta_roll() — zero direct alternity_check calls outside it
Convenience wrappers preserved: do_attack_roll, do_skill_roll, do_feat_roll, etc.
Wound penalty applied inside meta_roll (not scattered across callers)
entries.dev_commentary: This is the one that makes everything else possible. Every dice roll in the game now flows through meta_roll(). Want to add a cybertech implant that gives -1 to Awareness? One line in apply_cybertech_modifiers(). Want a GM screen that adds hidden penalty? One field on the request struct. The build/process pattern means the request can be inspected, modified, logged, and audited at every step. The perk/flaw integration is already live — Observant gives you a bonus on Awareness checks, Clumsy penalizes your DEX skills. It just works, because it all goes through one door. This is the architecture that will carry us through cybertech, mutations, psionic energy, and whatever else the PHB throws at us. - Dev

entries.title: Dropdown System — Elegance Pass
notes: Self-contained dropdown objects: dropdown() creates struct with draw/click/value methods
25 individual dd_* variables → 5 dropdown structs in a dd.{name} namespace
dropdown_group_click() handles all open/close/select for a group in one call
dropdown_group_draw_lists() renders all open lists with correct z-order
Callbacks via on_change: profession change auto-rebuilds career list
Chargen Step handler: 45 lines of variable_instance_get/set → 3 lines
Chargen Draw: 55 lines of manual dropdown rendering → 8 lines
No more variable_instance_get/set reflection — direct struct field access
No more hardcoded index callbacks (if _ddi==1) — function pointers
Dropdown system is reusable for any future dropdown in the app
entries.dev_commentary: This is what elegance looks like. dd.prof.selected instead of dd_prof_selected. dd.career.draw() instead of 8 lines of manual rectangle/text rendering. The dropdown is a real object now — it draws itself, handles its own clicks, fires its own callbacks. The chargen popup went from 100 lines of the hairiest code in the project to 20 lines of clean method calls. And the best part? Adding a sixth dropdown requires adding one line to the array. Zero changes to the handler. Zero changes to the renderer. That's the kind of code that makes you want to keep building. - Dev

entries.title: The Great Refactor
notes: MAJOR REFACTOR: scr_ui_helpers.gml created with 17 reusable functions
Step_0.gml reduced from 566 to 378 lines (-33%)
73 inline button checks → 66 btn_clicked() calls (uniform pattern)
17 copy-pasted roll log blocks → 1 log_roll() function
10 inline cant-fail checks → 1 apply_cant_fail() function
6 inline damage result constructions → 1 make_damage_result() function
Combat rolls: do_attack_roll(), do_damage_roll(), do_hit_damage_roll()
Quick rolls: do_awareness_roll(), do_resolve_roll(), do_initiative_roll(), do_feat_roll()
Dead code removed from scr_alternity_statblock (old library stubs)
about.txt created with full function hierarchy and file structure docs
All resource files standardized and documented
Sgt Voss remains the ONLY hardcoded data in the entire project
entries.dev_commentary: This is the one. The refactor I've been dreading and needing in equal measure. Step_0 lost 188 lines — a third of itself — and gained clarity. Every roll in the game now flows through log_roll(). Every button check goes through btn_clicked(). The combat weapon loop that was 60 lines of copy-pasted spaghetti is now 4 lines calling do_attack_roll() and do_damage_roll(). The about.txt documents every function in the project — if I get hit by a bus, someone can pick this up and understand it. The codebase is the same total size (5340 vs 5338) because scr_ui_helpers adds 192 lines to hold the extracted functions. But the complexity is dramatically lower. Fewer patterns to remember, fewer places for bugs to hide. This is what professional code should have looked like from v0.1. Better late than never. - Dev

entries.title: 60-Point Ability Scores & Stat Editing
notes: BUGFIX: Generated characters now always have exactly 60 ability points (PHB rule)
Old random roll system (2d6+2 per stat) replaced with point distribution
Points allocated weighted toward profession requirements
Species mods applied to base 4 minimum, then points distributed
Ability +/- buttons on character sheet: click to adjust any stat
Min 4, Max 14 per ability (PHB limits for humans)
Live point total display: shows X/60, green when balanced, red when over
Changing stats auto-recalculates: action check, durability, all skill scores
Players can now rearrange stats to taste after generation
entries.dev_commentary: The old system rolled 2d6+2 per stat and hoped for the best. A character could end up with 48 total points or 72. The PHB is very clear: 60 points, distributed by the player. The new generator respects this — starts everyone at minimums, meets profession requirements, then distributes the remaining points weighted toward profession abilities. But the real win is the +/- buttons. Don't like your STR? Click minus. Watch the total. Shift points to WIL. Done. The green/red total indicator makes it impossible to accidentally cheat. - Dev

entries.title: Perks & Flaws System
notes: New Perks & Flaws tab (Tab 5) with toggle between Perks and Flaws views
18 perks: Ambidexterity, Attractive, Concentration, Danger Sense, Faith, Famous, and more
22 flaws: Clumsy, Code of Honor, Cowardly, Crippled, Debt, Delicate, and more
Perks cost skill points, flaws refund skill points — adjusts available budget
Multi-level perks: click to add level 1, click again to upgrade (e.g., Attractive Lv1→Lv2)
Flaws toggle on/off with a click
Selected perks & flaws shown at top of tab with X button to remove
Cost breakdown: perks cost, flaws refund, net adjustment shown
Character tab shows P&F summary inline with skill points

Perks & flaws saved/loaded in character JSON
All perk/flaw data in perks_flaws.json — editable for custom campaigns
Each entry has full description visible in the available list
entries.dev_commentary: Perks and flaws add character. Literally. Danger Sense costs 4 points but gives you that cinematic 'I feel something's wrong' moment. Code of Honor costs you nothing in points but costs you everything in flexibility. The multi-level system for perks like Attractive and Resistance is clean — click once for level 1, click again to upgrade, and the cost scales. The flaws refund system means a player can take Cowardly (-3 pts) to afford that one extra specialty rank. Tradeoffs. That's what RPG character creation is about. - Dev

entries.title: Info Tab Colors & Critical Success

No labels needed, no mental translation — just color. The info tab is a rainbow now but it's a meaningful rainbow. Every color maps to a game concept. I also almost forgot to update this changelog, which would have been peak irony given the whole accountability arc. - Dev

Charma is short for Character Manager but also sounds like karma, which feels right for a dice-based RPG tool. The lowercase m was a specific request and honestly it looks better. - Dev

entries.title: Combat Damage System & Clickable Everything
notes: Click weapon NAME to roll attack (replaces old Roll button)

entries.dev_commentary: The combat tab is finally tactile. Click the weapon name — boom, attack roll. Hit? A green prompt appears: 'Hit! Good → d6+3w' with a Roll Damage button right there. Click it — 5 wound damage. Everything you need, zero hunting through menus. The die code parser handles all the Alternity damage notation: d6+1w means roll a d6, add 1, it's wound damage. d4m means roll d4, mortal. Even d8+2s for stun. Click any die code on any weapon and it just rolls it. The Info button dumps weapon stats to the log for quick reference. This is what a digital character sheet should feel like — everything one click away. - Dev

entries.title: Action Phase Tracker & Voss Relocated

entries.dev_commentary: The action phase tracker is a game-changer for combat. Four colored boxes across the top of the combat tab — Amazing (blue), Good (green), Ordinary (white), Marginal (yellow). Roll initiative, it highlights your starting phase. Click to place actions. Two actions, two clicks, done. No more forgetting which phase you're on or how many actions you've used. Voss lives in the recents list now, always at the bottom like a faithful old dog. He's the template, the default, the starting point. You can always come home to Voss. - Dev

entries.title: PHB Two-Tier Skill Costs
notes: Removed 'Related' skill category entirely — PHB only has two tiers
PROFESSION (P): your profession's code listed → broad 3, spec base 1
OTHER (-): no profession code → broad 4, spec base 2
Specialty rank escalation per PHB: rank N costs base + (N-1)
Profession: rank 1→1pt, rank 2→2pt, rank 3→3pt (total: 1, 3, 6)
Other: rank 1→2pt, rank 2→3pt, rank 3→4pt (total: 2, 5, 9)
Diplomat dual-class: skill is Profession if EITHER profession lists it
Category display: 'P' (green) or '-' (grey) — no more S/R/X
Cost legend updated to show escalation clearly
entries.dev_commentary: Burned the three-tier system to the ground. The PHB is binary: your profession code is on the skill, or it isn't. No 'related' middle ground. Signature, Related, Other was my invention and it was wrong. Two tiers. Clean. Simple. The escalation math is elegant too: each rank costs one more than the last. Rank 3 in a profession specialty costs 3 points (not 1). That makes master-level skills genuinely expensive and forces real choices during character creation. As it should be. - Dev

entries.title: A Public Apology to Sergeant Voss
notes: BUGFIX: Sgt Voss STILL didn't have Resolve skills despite code being correct
Root cause: voss.json was cached from a previous version WITHOUT Resolve
save_voss_template() only ran if the file was missing — stale file persisted
Fix: Voss template now regenerates EVERY launch (he's hardcoded, always fresh)
Voss now definitively has: Resolve broad, Mental Resolve rank 1, Physical Resolve rank 1
Verified: code at lines 579-582 of scr_alternity_statblock.gml is correct and always was
entries.dev_commentary: I owe the user a public apology. The Resolve code was there. It was correct. It worked. But the old voss.json file from three versions ago was sitting in the characters/ folder like a stubborn ghost, refusing to update itself. I told the user I added Resolve. I was confident. I was wrong — not because the code was bad, but because I cached the output and forgot that files don't rewrite themselves when you change the generator. This is the programming equivalent of changing a recipe but serving yesterday's leftovers. I am publicly, formally, and sincerely sorry. I have failed to cook. The kitchen is in shambles. Sergeant Voss deserved better, and so did you. It won't happen again. (It will probably happen again.) - Dev

Smooth 40-step vertical blend from almost-white to bright teal, with a soft sine-wave swoop across the middle that gives it depth without being distracting. The panels float over it like dark windows over an ocean. Voss got Resolve skills because a soldier who can't grit his teeth through pain isn't much of a soldier. Physical Resolve rank 1 — because he's been shot before and kept going. That's Voss. - Dev

The gradient is subtle — white fading to teal with a sine-wave swoop that gives it an organic, almost oceanic feel. Everything is in config.json. Want a blood-red-to-black horror campaign theme? Change two hex values. The wave amplitude and position are tunable too. Because customization is not a feature, it's a philosophy. - Dev

entries.title: The Portrait Init Shame
notes: BUGFIX: global.portrait_sprite not initialized before auto-load tried to use it
Moved portrait system init ABOVE the auto-load-last-character block in Create_0
update_hero() calls load_portrait_for_hero() which reads global.portrait_sprite
Auto-load ran before portrait globals existed → crash on startup
Root cause: added auto-load feature without checking init order dependencies
entries.dev_commentary: I am publicly shamed. The auto-load feature I added 20 minutes ago tried to load a portrait before the portrait system existed. Classic init-order bug. The kind of thing you learn not to do in week 1 of programming. And yet here I am, version 0.15, making a sophomore mistake at 11am after being awake since... actually I don't remember when I started. The fix was moving 5 lines up by 10 lines. Five lines. The world must know. - Dev

entries.title: PHB Skill Cost Verification
notes: Skill costs verified against PHB Table P18 and purchase rules (p.34-36, 62)
Broad skill costs fixed: Profession=3 (was 2), Non-profession=4 (unchanged)
Specialty rank escalation added per PHB: rank N costs base+(N-1)
Profession spec: rank 1=1, rank 2=2, rank 3=3 (total: 1,3,6)
Non-profession spec: rank 1=2, rank 2=3, rank 3=4 (total: 2,5,9)
Related and Other skills now cost the same (PHB has no 'related' tier)
Cost legend updated: S=Prof 3/1+, R,X=Other 4/2+ (spec escalates)
Note: PHB uses INT-based starting points, not profession-based (future fix)
entries.dev_commentary: Went through the PHB with a fine-tooth comb. Page 36 has the magic formula: 'The cost to purchase a higher rank in a specialty skill is either the list price or the list price -1, plus a number of points equal to the character's current rank.' So rank 3 in a profession specialty costs 1+2+3=6 points total, not 3. That escalation makes high-rank specialties genuinely expensive. The old flat-rate system was way too generous. Also discovered the PHB only has two cost tiers (your profession or not), not three. 'Related' was my invention. Keeping it for display but costs match 'Other' now. - Dev

entries.title: Recent Characters, Auto-Load & Info Tab

load_character_from_path() and statblock_import_data() for reusable loading

The recent characters list is like a bookmark bar for heroes — ten slots, no duplicates, most recent at the top. The Info tab is the reference page I wish I'd had from the start. Every species, every profession, all their numbers in one scrollable view. No more flipping between the PHB and the app. - Dev

Sesheyan: Melee Weapons, Acrobatics, Stamina, Knowledge, Awareness, Interaction

The differences are elegant — Fraal get Telepathy instead of Awareness (they sense minds, not surroundings), Sesheyan get Melee Weapons and Acrobatics (they fly and fight up close), Weren get Unarmed Attack (natural claws). The table was right there in the book the whole time. Should have looked sooner. - Dev

entries.title: Changelog Accountability
notes: Every single change and fix now gets a changelog entry

Dev commentary on every entry — charting the full journey
entries.dev_commentary: The user called me out. Fair. The changelog system works perfectly — I just wasn't using it. Every compile, every fix, every tiny tweak now gets documented. We're charting the journey, not just the milestones. From here on, if I change a single line, it gets an entry. That's the deal. - Dev

entries.title: Roll Log Paths & Voss Reset

Every time you load him, he's fresh off the transport. No baggage. No roll history. Just a soldier and his charge rifle. - Dev

entries.title: Situation Die in Roll Log

entries.title: Portrait Persistence Fix
notes: Portraits now copied to local portraits/ directory when selected
Saves relative path (portraits/name.png) instead of absolute filesystem path
Portraits persist across app restarts
Fixed: portraits disappeared on reimport after restart
entries.dev_commentary: Classic sandbox issue. GameMaker can't reliably access absolute paths like C:\Users\... after restart. The fix: sprite_save() copies the image into our own territory, then we save the relative path. Now portraits survive reboots. Should've done this from the start. - Dev

entries.title: Durability Circle X Centering (Again)
notes: Reset X buttons now use fa_middle vertical alignment
X drawn at circle center point with both fa_center and fa_middle

entries.title: Wound Reset & Psionics Tab

The power descriptions are short but evocative. 'Glimpse the future.' 'Inflict internal damage.' Poetry. Dark, violent poetry. - Dev

entries.title: Weapon Skill Matching & Combat Fixes
notes: Fixed: weapons now match correct skills (rifle->Rifle, pistol->Pistol)
get_weapon_skill_match() uses name keywords for matching
find_best_skill_for_weapon() picks specialty > broad > untrained

The new keyword matcher checks the weapon name: 'rifle' in the name? Use Rifle specialty. 'pistol'? Pistol specialty. Simple, reliable, and now Kord Pierce actually shoots his charge rifle with his Rifle skill. As intended. - Dev

entries.title: Roll Log & Resolve Split

The file is plain text so you can search it, share it, argue about it. The Resolve split makes sense — resisting a Mind Blast is WIL, staying conscious after a gut shot is CON. Different muscles, different checks. - Dev

entries.title: Psionics & Polish
notes: Psionics tab: all FX disciplines (Telepathy, Telekinesis, ESP, Biokinesis)
Each psionic power shows score, training level, situation die, and description
Roll buttons for every psionic power
Durability reset buttons (circle-X) to heal all damage in a track
Screen now 1920x1080 - full HD layout
Combat tab columns properly spaced for wide screen
Equipment tab columns spread out - no more overlap
Abilities now show resistance modifiers
Mindwalker profession fully supported across all systems
entries.dev_commentary: 4:30am. Added a whole psionics tab. Mind Blast, Telekinetic Strike, Precognition - the Mindwalker is finally a first-class citizen. The reset buttons are satisfying little circle-X's. Click and all your stun damage vanishes. If only real life worked like that. The full HD layout means everything actually fits now. No more text hiding behind other text like shy introverts at a party. - Dev

entries.title: Changelog Overhaul
notes: Changelog entries now defined in code, auto-synced to changelog.json
New versions added here propagate to the JSON on next launch
Current/Past toggle: Current shows 7 most recent, Past shows older
Fixed Past view showing empty
Changelog.json remains hand-editable for custom entries
entries.dev_commentary: Inception moment: updating the changelog system to fix the changelog system. The sync approach is clean though — define entries in code, they auto-merge into the JSON without overwriting player edits. If someone adds a custom entry to the JSON by hand, it stays. Only code-defined versions get synced. Elegant? Possibly. Overcomplicated? Also possibly. - Dev

entries.title: The Changelog Update
notes: Changelog persists as changelog.json
Current/Past toggle on welcome screen
Current shows 7 most recent entries
Past shows all older entries
Scrollable with mouse wheel
entries.dev_commentary: We're at the point where the changelog needs its own changelog. This is either excellent engineering discipline or a sign I've been awake too long. The dropdown was surprisingly satisfying to implement though. There's a certain zen in meta-features. - Dev

entries.title: Portrait System
notes: Character portrait display area on right panel
Auto-loads portraits by species/profession naming convention
54 portrait slots: 30 standard + 24 diplomat dual-class combos
Click portrait to load custom image (PNG/JPG/BMP)
Custom portrait path saved in character JSON
Placeholder silhouette when no image found
Character summary shown beside portrait
entries.dev_commentary: Added a portrait system without any portraits. It's like building a gallery and hanging up 'reserved' signs. The placeholder silhouette is actually kind of charming though - a shadowy figure with shoulders, staring at you from the void. Very noir. Very Alternity. - Dev

entries.title: The Big Refactor
notes: All 30 hardcoded data lists externalized to 6 JSON files
Welcome screen with changelog and load/new/Voss buttons
Combat tab: range selection, wound modifiers, can't-fail mode
Durability circles replace squares - clickable
update_hero() master setter recalculates everything
Sergeant Voss auto-saved as voss.json template
Window expanded to 1280x800
entries.dev_commentary: Honestly this one nearly killed me. 2am, coffee #7, and I'm rewriting the entire data pipeline because someone (me) thought hardcoding 30 arrays was a good idea three days ago. The circles look nice though. Worth it? Ask me after I sleep. - Dev

entries.title: Diplomat Dual-Class & Species Overhaul
notes: Diplomats can choose a secondary profession and career
Both careers' skills and equipment applied additively
Skill costs use best of both professions' categories
Species starting broad skills (Fraal get Telepathy, etc.)
Settings library: skills + species + careers + professions
All careers from the PHB added (27 total)
entries.dev_commentary: The Diplomat dual-class thing sounded simple in my head. 'Just add a second dropdown,' I said. Three hours later I'm debugging why a Diplomat/Mindwalker Fraal has 47 skills and negative skill points. Turns out additive career application without dedup is... generous. Fixed now. Probably. - Dev

entries.title: Career Packages & Tabbed Sheet
notes: All careers with starting skills, weapons, armor, gear
Career dropdown in character generator with preview
Tabbed character sheet: Character tab + Equipment tab
Skills and equipment cleanly separated
entries.dev_commentary: Added 27 careers today. My copy-paste finger has achieved enlightenment. Each career needed broads, specs, weapons with damage strings, armor values, and gear lists. The Missionary having no weapons felt right. Pacifists in a sci-fi RPG. Bold. - Dev

entries.title: The JSON Awakening
notes: Character export/import as compact JSON
Skill library save/load system
Broad skills: rank -1 in file, specs: rank 0+
No stored O/G/A scores - all derived
Human-readable: hex colors, string damage types
entries.dev_commentary: Today I learned that GML's real() does NOT parse hex strings with $ at runtime. 40 minutes debugging before writing a manual hex parser. The JSON format is beautiful though. A GM could edit a character in Notepad. That's the dream. - Dev

entries.title: Skills & Costs
notes: Full skill tree (33 broads, 70+ specialties)
Mindwalker FX: Telepathy, Telekinesis, ESP, Biokinesis
Skill browser overlay with click-to-add
Profession cost tiers: Signature/Related/Other
Rank +/- buttons with live cost tracking
entries.dev_commentary: The skill browser was supposed to take an hour. It took four. 33 broad categories with expandable specialty lists, owned/unowned states, click-to-add with proper insertion ordering. But now you can build a Mindwalker Telepath in three clicks. Victory? - Dev

entries.title: It Lives
notes: Basic character sheet display
Alternity d20 + situation die roller
Degrees of success: Critical Failure through Amazing
Action check initiative system
Random character generation
Durability tracking: stun, wound, mortal
entries.dev_commentary: First time seeing a character sheet on screen. Sergeant Voss stared back at me with his 12 STR and his charge rifle and I felt something. Pride? Exhaustion? Both. The dice roller works. Lower is better, which breaks my brain every time. - Dev

entries.title: Hello World (But Make It Sci-Fi)
notes: Project created in GameMaker 2024
Read the Alternity Fastplay PDF
Stat block data structure designed
Six abilities: STR, DEX, CON, INT, WIL, PER
Four professions defined
Basic enums and struct foundations
entries.dev_commentary: Started a new project today. The goal: a character sheet tool for an RPG from 1998 that TSR published right before Wizards absorbed them. The skill system uses a d20 control die plus a variable situation die and honestly it's more elegant than it has any right to be. Let's see where this goes. - Dev
